text.content:
Book of the Wider World — p.200-209

Stonetop sits on a bluff overlooking the Great Wood, which stretches hundreds of miles to the east. Few villagers dare to even cross the Stream (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eP9K8MXP1j3L6WZk]{396}) that flows near the base of the bluff. Hunters, trappers, and foragers go deeper, but even they venture a day or two into the forest at most.

Impressions

Always

  • Massive trunks, reaching up up up,
  • some bigger around than houses
  • Pale sky glimpsed through the canopy
  • Trees bending, creaking, rustling in the wind
  • A skittering in the distance
  • A sense of smallness, of being swallowed up by something ancient and vast

Spring

  • Feet slipping on mud, rotting leaves
  • Drip drip dripping of snowmelt, rain, mist
  • Pale buds and early blooms, blue and
  • yellow and violet
  • Hopeful swell of birdsong

The terrain is hilly and rugged, laced with small streams and dotted with ponds. The trees are a mix of deciduous and evergreen. In some spots, the trees grow tightly, surrounded by thick brush so that one can barely walk. In others, massive old growth trees dominate, the space between them sparse and open.

Summer

  • Brilliant, overwhelming green
  • Drone of insects buzzing, birds chirping
  • Oppressive heat and humidity

Autumn

  • A world aflame with reds and golds
  • Crunch of needles and leaves underfoot
  • Stark long shadows cast by a low sun
  • Sharp smell of cold, damp air

Winter

  • A frosty coating, a deathly stillness
  • Shocking white of snow drifts
  • Trees like boney fingers
  • Surprising chirp and rustle of finches

The Compact

For generations, the people of Stonetop have kept their promise to the Forest Folk (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.GaDYK1VEJEi6tHDa]{150}): they never cut down a living tree in the Great Wood. The villagers gather deadfall, but timber comes from elsewhere.

  • According to the elders, when did someone last break the compact? What
  • happened to them?
  • Who’s been pushing to start harvesting trees, since the Forest Folk seem to have vanished?

What will happen if someone fells a living tree? That’s for you to decide. Maybe…

… they suffer the Forest’s Wrath (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.QA1aP1FeKoG3h6Ev]{315});
… they risk releasing an evil spirit, held captive by the tree’s roots;
… the trees house the spirits of dead Forest Folk, and they’ve just created a specter (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{79}); or
… nothing—unless the Forest Folk return.

Questions

  • Who in your family used to ply the
  • Wood? How did they lose their nerve?
  • What did you see deep in the Wood
  • that you’ve never told to another?
  • Who did you lose to the Wood? How?
  • Of all the stories you’ve heard of the Wood, which one scares you most?

Places

  • The Golden Oak (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.qQEoRtdTq1COD9gw]{186})
  • The Maw (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FRtwmW1dhGs00kZH]{281})
  • Red groves (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.QA1aP1FeKoG3h6Ev]{314})
  • The Stream (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eP9K8MXP1j3L6WZk]{396})
  • Vor Svetelik (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.CEgJVcGKz5wPubOg]{468})

Hooks

  • A villager wanders off into the Wood for unknown reasons
  • A hunter stumbles home, having
  • narrowly escaped some new danger
  • A southern scholar arrives in Stonetop,
  • seeking a guide into the Great Wood
  • A hunter or trapper who the PCs care about hasn’t returned home
  • Trappers are catching strange game: sickly, mutated, and wrong

Steading improvement

  • GREAT WOOD TIMBER Requires all of the following:
  • Proof that trees can be felled without dire consequence
  • A crew of brave lumberjacks
  • A skilled carpenter, working full time
  • Pulling Together once, over a course of a few weeks, to generate the first load of timber.

When you mark all the requirements, increase Fortunes by 1 and add “Timber from the Great Wood” to the Resources list.

Henceforth, the steading has a ready supply of timber for improvements and other projects, and it consumes 1 less Surplus every winter.

Terrain

Pick 1 or combine 2, or have someone roll the Die of Fate (d12).

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.P8DPXNknsC2nxaFs]{1d12}Terrain

1Pond, wetland, or lake
2Creek, stream, or river
3Rocky outcropping, cave(s)
4-5Clearing, meadow, sparse trees
6-7Dense thicket
8-9Swath of one type of tree
10Briars, thorns, nettles
11Slope, hill, ridge, ravine
12Single, notable tree

If you like, roll 1d6 for size/scope/intensity.

Discoveries

For any of these, pick 1 or have someone roll the Die of Fate.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.zuzTU9ZPifpozKwV]{1d6}Discovery

1A change of terrain (previous page) and roll again
2A chance for insight into a threat or danger (e.g. tracks that reveal numbers, whereabouts, etc.)
3game (see below)
4Useful or valuable flora (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.y0RKxmPHaMCuP9r6]{458})
5A crinwin (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FdpWHP3EYMLyOPEB]{58}), Fae (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.YMV8KIodnoZL71Jl]{94}) or spirit of the wild (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356}), curious or indifferent
6A point of interest (next column)

1d6 game

1-3 Mostly harmless but prone to bolt: deer, fowl, rabbits, etc. 4-5 Big and dangerous if provoked: boars (page 204), wisents (page 209), tal gerdwyr (page 209), etc.

6Deadly and easily provoked: cave bears (page 205), ceirwmawr (page 205), raselbaedd (page 209), etc.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.cGJZYIkhCe0VhAeu]{1d6}Point of interest

1A place corrupted by the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}) or the Fomoraij (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.z8rAsGkDl5lJd8GG]{136})
2A place haunted by death and the undying (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{68})
3A place bearing traces of the Forest Folk (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.GaDYK1VEJEi6tHDa]{150})
4A place sacred to the spirits of the wild (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356})
5A Fae domain (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.YMV8KIodnoZL71Jl]{102}) or waystone (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.aqVGWpa7uuuO4GVV]{478})
6A ruin or artifact of the Green Lords (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.gnP4Q10EDePdvgqz]{210})

If it’s been there for at least 10 years, it’s likely marked by Forest Folk glyphs (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.GaDYK1VEJEi6tHDa]{152}).

Dangers

Hazards

  • Getting lost
  • Treacherous terrain (loose footing, mudslides, sinkholes, deadfall, etc.)
  • Swarm (bees, wasps, midges, bats, etc.)
  • Tall tree to climb, and a reason to climb it

Monsters

  • Crinwin (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FdpWHP3EYMLyOPEB]{58})
  • Fae (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.YMV8KIodnoZL71Jl]{94})
  • Spirits of the Wild (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356})
  • Chimerae and other remnants of the Green Lords (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.gnP4Q10EDePdvgqz]{210})
  • Servants of the Fomoraij (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.z8rAsGkDl5lJd8GG]{136})
  • The ghosts (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{76}) or revenants (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{80}) of those who died in the Wood, like the ﻿Gwraig Wen (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{78})
  • A copse of dool trees and its resident
  • spirits (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{73})
  • The Pale Hunter and his hounds
  • (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.MCe2YgHD24ptZJrG]{292})
  • The Crombil (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.ABlsk5058TiGELkB]{62}), should it wake
  • One of the many beasts that inhabit the Wood
Boar
Group, gluttonous
HP 10; Armor 0
Damage gore d8 (hand, messy, 1 piercing)
Instinct to protect their territory
  • Relentlessly charge their victim, over and over
  • Eat just about anything
  • Ignore pain or injury
Butcherbird
Horde, tiny, organized
HP 1; Armor 1 (tiny)
Damage peck d6-2 (hand, messy, 2 piercing)
Instinct to feast on bloody, bloody meat
  • Grab on and stab stab stab
  • Fend off any who would steal the flock’s meal
  • Scent blood from miles away
About the size of a woodpecker, with black
and wine-red plumage, flitting about in
small flocks (2d6+4). Their laugh will make
your blood run cold. But don’t worry. They’ll
get your blood flowing again, real soon.
Something interesting: Butcherbirds can
smell fresh blood from miles away, and
might descend on a hunter’s kill to gorge.
Something useful: They can smell the
blood of their own, and shun it; smeared
butcherbird blood is an excellent repellent.
Cave bear
Solitary, large, cautious
HP 16; Armor 1 (fur, hide)
Damage claws, bite, hug d10+4 (hand, close, forceful, messy, 1 piercing)
Instinct to fill its belly, to protect its young
  • Rend, maul, crush
  • Move with surprising speed and grace
  • Sniff out food, or trouble
Great big silver-brown things, eating any-
thing they can dig up or catch. They mostly
hibernate through winter, and get especial-
ly bold and ornery in autumn as they try to
fatten up. They’ve been known to wake up
and forage even in winter, though.
They lair in big caves, usually two mates
and maybe their cubs. Out and about,
the bulls are usually found on their own;
the sows on their own, or with their cubs.
They’re not usually aggressive towards
people—unless surprised, extra hungry, or
protecting the cubs. Then? Watch out.
Ceirwmawr
Solitary, large, terrifying, fearless
HP 16; Armor 0
Damage hoof d10+1 (close) or antlers d10+3 (reach, forceful)
Special quality awe-inspiring antlers
Instinct to tolerate no disrespect
  • Toss aside a barrier or foe
  • Suffer harm without flinching
  • Make a show of strength
Majestic. Proud. Antlers so large you can
scarce believe how deftly it moves through
the woods. They are the royalty of deer. It
is said that only the Pale Hunter can give
them chase, and that any mortal who dares
to stalk them quickly finds the tables turned.

Questions

  • What special property does folklore say
  • the ceirwmawr’s antlers possess?
  • What dire fate is said to befall those who slay a ceirwmawr without the Pale Hunter’s permission?
Cougar
Solitary, stealthy, cautious, athletic
HP 12, Armor 0
Damage bite d10 (hand, grabby)
Special qualities keen night vision and sense of smell
Instinct to catch prey unawares
  • Move into position, silently and unseen
  • Pounce on prey, dragging them down (forceful)
  • Bite down and rake (messy)
They don’t usually hunt people. But when
game is scarce, or some fool provokes
them, they sometimes get a taste for
human blood. Even then, they mostly leave
groups alone, waiting for someone to fall
behind or wander off. Mostly.
Cwisarff
Solitary, small, stealthy, cautious
HP 10; Armor 0
Damage venomous bite d10 (close)
Instinct to defend its current spot
  • Wait patiently to spring on prey
  • Hiss menacingly at a threat
  • Bite quickly and ready to strike again
The vaunted “tree adder,” most lethal
serpent in the Wood, up to 6 feet long and
dripping with venom. In warm months,
they hunt and bask in the canopy or sunny
clearings. In colder months, they hide in
hollows and under logs, going into torpor
in winter. They don’t hunt people, but they
don’t fear them either.
When you are bitten by a cwisarff, mark
a debility in addition to taking damage.
Until the poison is treated, you can’t regain
HP and are in danger of further damage and debility.
Cynddaraig
Solitary, large, terrifying, fearless
HP 20; Armor 2 (scales)
Damage three-inch long teeth d10+3 (close, reach, grabby, messy, 3 piercing, advantage)
Instinct to dominate its territory
  • Roar and unfurl its quills, making even brave warriors quail
  • Bite a limb clean off
  • To lose itself in primal fury
The mighty “rage drake,” almost 10 feet
tall and 20 feet from nose to tip of tail. Its
jaws are full of knives. Blood-red patterns
surround its eyes. A frill of feathered quills
sprouts from its neck and runs down its
back. Maybe a half-dozen of them stalk
the whole Great Wood, and one hasn’t
been spotted near Stonetop for generations
(Tor be praised).
Something interesting: They prey mostly
on large game (wisents, tal gerdwyr, etc.).
They’ll attack people, but rarely hunt them.
Something useful: They attack people to
establish dominance, seeing us as rivals. An
appropriate offering or show of obeisance
might calm one enough for you to get the
hell out of there.
Feathered drake
Group, stealthy, organized
HP 6; Armor 1 (feathers, scales)
Damage boney crest d8+2 (close, forceful), talon & teeth d8+2 (hand, messy, 1 piercing)
Special qualities flightless
Instinct to spring on unwary prey
  • Burst out of the brush
  • Bash prey to the ground, senseless
  • Surround downed prey, and rend
Bigger than a grown man, with a talon
yea-long on each foot and that ridge on
their heads they use to go crashing through
the undergrowth. They might follow a
party for days, waiting for them to let their
guard down or for someone to fall behind.
Then? Bam.
Their feathers are much-sought by
merchants. You can harvest a bundle
of drake feathers (fragile, Value 3) from a □
flock of 3-4 drakes.

Gylglyd vines

Damage strangling vines d6 (hand, grabby) Instinct to lure prey

  • Ensnare their senses with beauty
  • Lull their minds with euphoric scents
  • Slowly entwine them, unnoticed, and hold them fast

Thick, velvety green vines that drape entire groves or clearings, speckled with flowers of iridescent blue and pink and yellow. The mind reels to see them, wanting to move closer. The flowers give off the sweetest scent, erasing all cares. But the wary might notice strange bulges in the hanging vines, or white bone beneath the carpet.

Something interesting: Gylglyd flowers are prized by herbalists and apothecaries, but nearly impossible to cultivate.

Something useful: A kerchief soaked in musk can counter the euphoric effect.

Hagr
Solitary, large, terrifying
HP 16; Armor 1 (blubbery hide)
Damage meaty hands d10+3 (hand, close, grabby, forceful) or tree trunk d10+5 (reach, area, crude, forceful) or hurled heavy thing d10+3 (thrown, forceful)
Instinct to compulsively shape its environment
  • Fix someone with its evil eye, overwhelming them with fear
  • Pluck things from the ground as if weightless
  • Obsessively stack things to exacting standards
  • Throw a tantrum when things aren’t just right
Misshapen brutes with sagging flesh and
one bulging eye so filled with hate it’ll stop
you dead in your tracks. They spend their
days collecting fallen branches, looking
for just the right one to add to their home
of rotting logs. The others they pile neatly,
reverently. Perhaps they were servants of
the Green Lords, once bred to build, and
now, long ages hence, building still.
Nailadd
Group, cunning, stealthy
HP 6; Armor 2 (carapace)
Damage venomous bite d8 (hand)
Special qualities webs, prodigious leaps
Instinct to capture prey and slowly consume it
  • Leap on prey and knock it prone
  • Inject a paralytic venom
  • Drag helpless prey into the canopy and cocoon it
So far, these hound-sized spiders have
thankfully avoided hunters and trappers
in the Wood. They certainly haven’t come
near town. But they’re out there, rest
assured, and growing in numbers.
Something interesting: Nailadd venom
paralyzes prey, rather than killing it.
Something useful: You can harvest the
venom (d4 uses, dangerous, reload, Value 1)
from an intact corpse. When you coat a
blade in nailadd venom, it deals +1d4 extra
damage and numbs the wounded area.
Raselbaedd
Solitary, large
HP 20; Armor 4 (hide, unnatural resilience)
Damage gore d10+5 (hand, close, forceful, messy, 3 piercing)
Special qualities iron hide, always hungry, easily provoked, afraid of fire
Instinct to hold a grudge
  • Rip things apart: armor, weapons, flesh
  • Eat almost anything
  • Refuse to back off or let go, even when dying
  • Recover from even mortal wounds, over time
When a boar lives long enough, and
survives enough close calls, and dines year
after year on the roots and nuts of ferro
trees, it eventually becomes a raselbaedd.
The size of a horse, with tusks like short
spears and a hide that’s crisscrossed with
scars and that bristles with broken blades.
When you deal 2 damage or less to a rasel-
baedd, before armor, your weapon chips or
shatters on its hide.
A raselbaedd’s hide (immobile, Value 2) can
be harvested by a competent hunter with a
fine steel blade (2 piercing, Value 2). It can
then be fashioned into 1d4 raselbaedd □
cuirasses (2 armor, Value 3). Doing so
requires:
□ An exceptional tanner
□ An upgraded workshop with fine steel
tools (Value 3)
□ A season of labor per cuirass
Tal gerdwyr
Group, huge
HP 14; Armor 2 (feathers, scales)
Damage trample, kick d8+5 (close, forceful), tail sweep d8+5 (reach, area, forceful)
Special quality extremely long neck
Instinct to get annoyed
  • Bellow loudly
  • Smash down or through a barrier
  • Reach very, very high up
The Forest Folk would sometimes tell sto-
ries of the “tall walkers,” drakes with necks
like trees and bodies like small hills, draped
in feathers that look like vines and leaves.
Supposedly, they roam deep in the forest,
blithely chewing leaves from the canopy
and singing mournful songs to each other.
Wisent
Group, large, cautious
HP 10; Armor 0
Damage headbutt, gore, trample d8+3 (hand, close, forceful)
Instinct to protect the herd
  • Put itself between a threat and the young
  • Stampede (area, forceful, messy)
  • Leap a surprising height and distance, even from a standstill
Forest bison travel in herds of up to twenty,
but only the bulls typically fight. When
cornered, though, even the sows can be
dangerous due to their mass and strength.
Wolf
Group, organized, cautious
HP 6; Armor 0
Damage bite d8 (hand, grabby) Qualities keen sense of smell
Instinct to bring down the weakest prey
  • Follow prey for miles on end
  • Surround, flank, and harry
  • Clamp down, eventually crushing bone
